era_context: The early 7th century CE is a period of major geopolitical transformation. The Islamic movement under Prophet Muhammad is consolidating in Medina (the Hijra occurred in 622 CE, so in 621 CE Muhammad was still in Mecca). The Byzantine-Sasanid conflict dominates the Mediterranean and Near East. The Tang Dynasty in China is in its early consolidation phase (established 618 CE). Maya civilization in Mesoamerica is in its Classic period. This year falls just before the Hijra, a pivotal moment in world history.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of the world outside major empires

events.title: Islamic movement: Pre-Hijra period in Mecca
events.description: In 621 CE, Prophet Muhammad and his followers were still in Mecca, facing persecution from the Quraysh. The Islamic movement was in its early phase, with a small community of believers. Muhammad was preaching monotheism and social reform, which threatened the economic and religious interests of the Quraysh, who controlled the Ka'ba and benefited from pagan pilgrimage. The year 621 CE is significant in Islamic tradition as the year of the 'Night Journey' (Isra and Mi'raj), when Muhammad is said to have traveled to Jerusalem and ascended to heaven. However, the dating of this event is disputed by modern scholars. The community would migrate to Medina the following year (622 CE).
key_figures: Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (Muhammad's first wife, died c. 619 CE)

sources.name: Sirat Ibn Hisham (biography of Muhammad, compiled 9th century CE)
Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (9th century CE)

events.certainty_note: The dating of the 'Night Journey' to 621 CE is based on Islamic tradition compiled 150+ years after the event. Modern scholars debate the dating and historical basis of this event. No contemporary non-Islamic sources document this event. The general narrative of Islamic persecution in Mecca is attested in Islamic historiography, but specific events in 621 CE are not precisely dated.
cross_references: Part of early Islamic movement (610–632 CE)

events.title: Tang Dynasty: Consolidation and military campaigns
events.description: Emperor Gaozu of Tang continued consolidating Tang control over China in 621 CE. The Tang had recently defeated rival warlords and were establishing administrative structures. This period saw significant military activity, including campaigns against the Eastern Turks and other regional powers. The Tang were also recruiting talented military commanders and administrators. By 621 CE, the Tang position was secure enough to begin planning campaigns for southern unification.
key_figures: Li Yuan, Emperor Gaozu (Tang founder, r. 618–626 CE)
Li Shimin (future Emperor Taizong, military commander)

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang, compiled 945 CE)
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang, compiled 1060 CE)

events.certainty_note: The general chronology of Tang consolidation is secure, but specific events in 621 CE may lack detailed documentation. Dating is approximate to ±1–2 years.
cross_references: Part of Tang Dynasty consolidation (618–650 CE)

events.title: Sasanid-Byzantine conflict: Continued warfare
events.description: The Sasanid-Byzantine conflict continued in 621 CE with both empires engaged in prolonged warfare. The Sasanids held significant territorial advantages, controlling much of the Levant, Mesopotamia, and Egypt. The Byzantine Empire was in a defensive position, though Emperor Heraclius was beginning to reorganize forces for a counteroffensive. The conflict had devastated both empires economically and militarily, weakening their ability to resist future Arab expansion.

sources.name: Theophanes the Confessor, Chronicle (9th century CE)
Sebeos, Armenian History (7th century CE, near-contemporary)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of the conflict is well-documented, but specific events in 621 CE are less precisely dated. Sources agree on the broad chronology but differ on tactical details.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the 'Night Journey' to 621 CE is disputed by modern scholars. Some scholars place it earlier or later, or question its historical basis entirely. The event is attested only in Islamic tradition compiled 150+ years after the alleged occurrence. No contemporary non-Islamic sources document this event. For the Tang Dynasty, specific military campaigns in 621 CE are not well-documented in surviving sources. For the Byzantine-Sasanid conflict, the exact status of military operations in 621 CE is uncertain.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 621 CE is sparse and heavily weighted toward Islamic and Chinese sources. Islamic sources are abundant but compiled centuries after events and shaped by theological agendas. Chinese sources are well-preserved but may emphasize court narratives. Byzantine and Sasanid sources are fragmentary. Most of the world is undocumented for this year. The surviving record reflects the concerns and perspectives of literate, centralized states and likely omits the experiences of most of humanity.

graph_edges.note: Pre-Hijra Islamic movement in Mecca led to the Hijra to Medina
